title: Questionnaires and Structured Interviews
examTip: When evaluating questionnaires, always distinguish between closed questions (quantitative, high reliability, low validity) and open questions (qualitative, higher validity, harder to analyse). Examiners reward this distinction.
introTips: Questionnaires produce quantitative data from closed questions — but open questions give qualitative data.
High reliability (replicable) but often low validity (are people telling the truth?).
Always think about response rate — who doesn't respond and why that matters.
intro: Imagine you want to know what 10,000 teenagers think about social media — you can't interview all of them. That's why questionnaires exist. They let sociologists collect data from huge numbers of people at low cost, but the decisions made in designing them — what questions to ask, how to word them, what choices to offer — shape the answers you'll get before a single person has even read them.

keyPoints.heading: Questionnaire Design and Types
keyPoints.content: Questionnaires are structured research instruments with pre-set questions asked of all respondents in the same way. Closed questions offer fixed-choice responses (tick boxes, Likert scales ranging from 'strongly agree' to 'strongly disagree', yes/no). They produce quantitative data that is quick to analyse statistically. Open questions allow respondents to write free text answers, producing qualitative data that is richer but harder to code and analyse. Questionnaires can be delivered: by post or email (self-completion), online (e.g., SurveyMonkey), or handed out in person. Design principles matter enormously: use clear, unambiguous language; avoid leading questions ('Don't you think crime is getting worse?' — implies an expected answer); avoid double-barrelled questions ('Do you drink alcohol and smoke?' — what if the person does only one?); always conduct a pilot study first (a small-scale test with a sample of the target group to identify problems before the full study).
writeBullets: Closed questions — fixed choices (tick boxes, Likert scales) → quantitative data
Open questions — free text responses → qualitative data, harder to analyse
Delivered by post, email, online or in person
Avoid leading questions — 'Don't you think X?' implies a correct answer
Avoid double-barrelled questions — ask one thing at a time
Pilot study — small-scale test to identify problems before the main study
keyPoints.memTip: PLAN — Pilot study, Leading questions avoided, Ambiguity removed, Numbers from closed questions.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often lose marks by not being specific about question types — always distinguish closed (quantitative) from open (qualitative).

keyPoints.heading: Advantages of Questionnaires
keyPoints.content: Questionnaires offer major practical advantages that explain their widespread use in sociology and market research. Large samples: thousands of responses can be collected quickly and cheaply — online surveys in particular can reach huge numbers at almost no cost. Standardisation: all respondents answer the same questions in the same order, making responses directly comparable. Anonymity: respondents are more likely to answer honestly on sensitive topics (drug use, sexual behaviour, domestic violence) when their identity is protected. Quantitative data: closed questions produce data that can be analysed statistically — calculating percentages, running correlations, identifying patterns across groups. High reliability: another researcher using the same questionnaire should get very similar results — the method is replicable. No interviewer bias: because the researcher doesn't interact directly with respondents during completion, there is no risk that the researcher's appearance, accent, or manner influences answers.
writeBullets: Large samples quickly and cheaply — especially online surveys
Standardised — all respondents answer the same questions, allowing comparison
Anonymity — encourages honest answers on sensitive topics
Quantitative data from closed questions — allows statistical analysis
High reliability — replicable; another researcher gets similar results
No interviewer bias — researcher doesn't interact with respondents during completion
keyPoints.memTip: SCAN — Standardised, Cheap/large, Anonymity, No interviewer bias.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Reliability vs validity is a key distinction — questionnaires are high on reliability but often low on validity.

keyPoints.heading: Disadvantages of Questionnaires
keyPoints.content: Despite their practical advantages, questionnaires have serious weaknesses. Low response rate: postal surveys typically achieve only 30–40% return rates — meaning more people don't respond than do. This risks a biased sample: who doesn't respond? Often the very busy, the less literate, and the least engaged — potentially those whose views would differ most. Inflexibility: researchers cannot probe or clarify; if a respondent misunderstands a question, the researcher will never know. Lack of depth: closed questions capture surface answers, not the reasons, feelings, or context behind them. Social desirability effect (sometimes called the Hawthorne effect variant): respondents give socially acceptable answers rather than truthful ones — for example, claiming to recycle more than they do, or underreporting alcohol consumption. Assumes shared understanding: questions may mean different things to different respondents — cultural, educational, and language differences can distort responses. Low validity: are people's self-reported behaviours accurate? People's actual behaviour often differs from what they claim.
writeBullets: Low response rate — postal surveys often only 30–40% returned
Biased sample — non-responders may differ systematically from responders
Inflexible — cannot probe or clarify misunderstood questions
Lacks depth — closed questions capture surface answers only
Social desirability effect — respondents give respectable rather than truthful answers
Low validity — self-reported behaviour often differs from actual behaviour
keyPoints.memTip: BLIND — Biased sample, Low response, Inflexible, No depth, Desirability effect.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Validity is the key weakness — questionnaires tell you what people say they do, not what they actually do.

keyPoints.heading: Structured Interviews
keyPoints.content: A structured interview is essentially a questionnaire delivered face-to-face or by telephone by an interviewer, who reads the same questions in the same order to every respondent. Advantages over postal questionnaires: higher response rate (harder to refuse someone face-to-face), the interviewer can clarify questions without probing or leading (making responses more accurate), and the interviewer can observe basic non-verbal responses. Disadvantages: considerably more expensive and time-consuming than self-completion questionnaires (interviewer time must be paid for); interviewer bias is possible — the interviewer's appearance, accent, ethnicity, or manner can influence how respondents answer (e.g., a respondent may give different answers about racist attitudes depending on the ethnicity of the interviewer). Structured interviews still lack depth — the fixed format prevents exploration of interesting or unexpected answers. The Crime Survey for England and Wales (CSEW), formerly the British Crime Survey, is a major example — it annually interviews 35,000+ households about victimisation in the past year (whether reported to police or not), making it a much more valid measure of crime than police-recorded statistics alone.
writeBullets: Structured interview — questionnaire delivered face-to-face or by phone by an interviewer
Higher response rate than postal — harder to refuse in person
Interviewer can clarify questions — improves accuracy without probing
Interviewer bias — interviewer's characteristics can influence responses
More expensive than self-completion — interviewer time costs money
CSEW (Crime Survey for England and Wales) — 35,000+ households annually, captures unreported crime
keyTerms: Crime Survey for England and Wales
keyPoints.memTip: Structured interviews = questionnaire with a human face — higher response, but bias risk.
keyPoints.tutor_note: CSEW is a real-world example of structured interviews at scale — it's also relevant to S4 (measuring crime).

tables.title: Questionnaires vs Structured Interviews
rows: Very large — thousands at low cost
Higher — harder to refuse in person
Possible — interviewer can clarify without probing
Crime Survey for England and Wales (35,000+ households annually)

0.question: A sociologist asks: 'Don't you think social media is harmful to young people?' What is wrong with this question?
options: It is a leading question — it implies the expected answer is 'yes'
It is a double-barrelled question — it asks two things at once
It is an open question — it should use a Likert scale instead
It has a low response rate — it should be sent by post
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. 'Don't you think X?' is a classic leading question — it steers the respondent towards agreeing. Good questionnaire design avoids this.
0.wrongFeedback: Look at the question again. It asks only one thing (about social media), so it's not double-barrelled. The problem is the wording 'Don't you think' — what does that signal to a respondent?

3.question: The Crime Survey for England and Wales (CSEW) uses structured interviews with 35,000+ households. Which of the following is a key advantage over police-recorded crime statistics?
options: It captures crimes that were never reported to the police
It uses open questions that produce qualitative data
It is cheaper to run than a postal questionnaire
It has no interviewer bias because it is conducted online
3.correctFeedback: Correct. The CSEW asks people whether they have been victims of crime, regardless of whether they reported it — so it captures unreported crime that is invisible in police statistics.
3.wrongFeedback: Think about why the CSEW was created in the first place. Police statistics only count crimes that were reported and recorded. What does the CSEW add to that picture?

commonMistakes: Saying questionnaires are always quantitative — open questions produce qualitative data too.
Confusing reliability and validity — questionnaires are high reliability (replicable) but low validity (people may not tell the truth).
Forgetting to mention response rate as a disadvantage — and the key problem that non-responders may be a biased group.
Saying 'interviewer bias' means the interviewer is deliberately biased — it's usually unconscious (the way they look, their accent, their tone).
Not linking questionnaires to positivism — the positivist preference for scientific, quantitative, replicable methods should always be mentioned in evaluation.
examTips: In 'evaluate questionnaires' questions, always pair reliability (strength) against validity (weakness) — this is the core trade-off.
If asked to design a questionnaire question, show you know what 'closed' and 'open' mean — and avoid the classic mistakes (leading, double-barrelled).
The CSEW is a real-world example worth memorising — use it in both Research Methods (S3) and Crime and Deviance (S4) questions.
revisionCardBullets: Questionnaires — structured research instrument with pre-set questions for all respondents
Closed questions — fixed choices → quantitative data; easy to analyse statistically
Open questions — free text → qualitative data; richer but harder to code
Leading questions — 'Don't you think X?' — imply the expected answer (must be avoided)
Double-barrelled questions — ask two things at once (must be avoided)
Pilot study — small test-run to find problems before the full study
Key advantage — large samples, cheap, anonymous, reliable, no interviewer bias
Key disadvantage — low response rate, inflexible, low validity, social desirability effect
Social desirability effect — respondents give respectable rather than honest answers
Structured interview — questionnaire delivered face-to-face by an interviewer
Interviewer bias — interviewer's characteristics influence respondent answers
CSEW — Crime Survey for England and Wales; 35,000+ households; captures unreported crime
